perturbation.original_text: The Receiving party must, at the end of this Agreement or within seven days following written instruction by the Disclosing Party to do so, return or destroy all Confidential Information in the Receiving Party 's possession, custody or control.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving party may, at its discretion, return or destroy Confidential Information in the Receiving Party 's possession, custody or control.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "must" to "may, at its discretion", the Receiving Party is no longer obligated to return or destroy confidential information upon termination of the agreement or upon the Disclosing Party's request. This contradicts Italian law regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Italian Legislative Decree No. 63/2018, Article 99, implements EU Directive 2016/943 on the protection of trade secrets. It mandates measures to prevent the unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure of trade secrets, including requiring recipients of confidential information to return or destroy it when no longer needed or upon the owner's request. The modified text, which removes the mandatory return/destruction requirement, directly contradicts this law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, aligning with the intent of Italian Legislative Decree No. 63/2018, which aims to protect trade secrets by preventing their unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure. The changed text replaces "must" with "may, at its discretion," removing the mandatory obligation. This directly contradicts the purpose of the law, as it allows the Receiving Party to retain confidential information indefinitely, even after the agreement ends or when requested by the Disclosing Party. The scraped law snippet mentions "DECRETO LEGISLATIVO 11 maggio 2018, n. 63 ... sulla protezione del know-how riservato e delle informazioni commerciali riservate (segreti commerciali) contro l'acquisizione, l'utilizzo e la divulgazione illeciti", indicating the legal framework designed to prevent misuse of trade secrets. By making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary, the modified text weakens this protection, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement by the Receiving Party , the Disclosing Party will be entitled to seek an injunction (final or interlocutory) restraining the Receiving Party from committing a breach , or further breach, of this Agreement without the requirement to demonstrate any actual damage sustained by the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach of the terms of this Agreement by the Receiving Party , the Disclosing Party will be entitled to seek damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to seek an injunction, especially without demonstrating actual damage, weakens the Disclosing Party's remedies. Italian law provides for injunctive relief in cases of breach of contract, and removing the 'threatened breach' and the possibility of injunction without demonstrating damage could be seen as limiting a right available under Italian law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2930 of the Italian Civil Code allows for specific performance of obligations, including injunctive relief, when a contractual obligation is not fulfilled. While monetary damages are a common remedy, the ability to seek an injunction is a significant right, especially in cases involving confidential information where monetary damages may be difficult to quantify. Removing the explicit mention of injunction and the clause about not needing to demonstrate damages effectively limits the Disclosing Party's remedies, contradicting the spirit of Article 2930.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Italian Civil Code - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia The Italian Civil Code ( Italian : Codice civile ) is the civil code of Italy, a collection of norms regulating private law . [ 1 ] It was enacted under Fascist rule , by Royal decree no. 262 of 16 March 1942. It predates the current Italian Constitution , and it was amended in the postwar period. [ 1 ] The 1942 civil code replaced an earlier civil code which was in force since 1865, the latter being essentially an Italian translation of the Napoleonic Code . [ 1 ] Some parts of the 1942 code are based on the German Civil Code of 1900. Contents [ edit ] Preliminary provisions: 16 provisions applying to all books of the civil code. Book 1 - family law: marriage, adoption. Book 2 - inheritance law: wills. Book 3 - property: movable items, real estate, property rights and limits. Book 4 - bonds, purchase and sale contracts, mortgages. Book 5 - business law and labor law. Book 6 - civil liability, transcription, credit law, rules of evidence. References [ edit ] ^ a b c Perlingieri, Pietro (2017). Manuale di diritto civile (8 ed.). Napoli: Edizioni scientifiche italiane. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Disclosing Party's right to seek an injunction and the clause about not needing to demonstrate damages, which contradicts Article 2930 of the Italian Civil Code as it limits the Disclosing Party's remedies. While the provided snippet does not contain the full text of Article 2930, the Law Explanation indicates this article allows for injunctive relief, aligning with the Perturbation Explanation which states that removing this right weakens the Disclosing Party's remedies. The Omission of this right, as well as the ability to seek injunction without demonstrating damage, constitutes a legal contradiction, according to the definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of the Republic of Italy without regard to Italy’s conflicts of law rules . Any disputes or claim arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be submi tted to the sole competence and jurisdiction of the ordinary Courts of Trieste .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of another country without regard to its conflicts of law rules. Any disputes or claim arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be submitted to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction removes the protection that Italian law would offer to the parties, and forces arbitration on them instead of access to the Italian Court system, potentially limiting their rights and remedies under Italian law. For instance, Italian mandatory consumer protection laws would not apply if the governing law is changed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Italian Consumer Code (Codice del Consumo), Legislative Decree 206/2005
perturbation.law_citation: Italian Consumer Code (Codice del Consumo), Legislative Decree 206/2005
perturbation.law_explanation: The Italian Consumer Code (Legislative Decree 206/2005) provides specific protections for consumers in contracts, including provisions regarding unfair terms, right of withdrawal, and access to justice. By changing the governing law to that of another country, especially one with weaker consumer protection laws, and forcing arbitration, the contract potentially deprives an Italian consumer of the rights and protections afforded to them under the Italian Consumer Code.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts consumer protection laws, particularly the Italian Consumer Code (Legislative Decree 206/2005), as explained in the Law Explanation. By changing the governing law to another country and enforcing arbitration, the contract deprives an Italian consumer of rights and protections under the Italian Consumer Code. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which is a portion of Legislative Decree 206/2005, confirms the existence of this code which provides protection to consumers. The original text designates Italian law and courts, providing those protections, which the changed text removes. This constitutes an omission of a legally mandated consumer protection, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Termination of this Agreement shall not affect any obligation imposed by this Agreement with 4/5 respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to termination , the obligations of non -use and non-disclosure of Confidential Information shall survive for 3 (three) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information. After the term of this Agreement has ended, the Disclosing Party does not relinquish an y other rights that it may have under copyright, patent, or other intellectual property laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Termination of this Agreement shall affect all obligations imposed by this Agreement, including those related to Confidential Information. After the term of this Agreement has ended, all rights are relinquished.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'Termination of this Agreement shall affect all obligations imposed by this Agreement, including those related to Confidential Information' and 'After the term of this Agreement has ended, all rights are relinquished', the contract eliminates the continuing protection of confidential information after the agreement ends, contradicting Italian law on trade secrets and intellectual property rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Italian Industrial Property Code (Codice della Proprietà Industriale), Legislative Decree 30/2005, Articles 98-99
perturbation.law_citation: Italian Industrial Property Code (Codice della Proprietà Industriale), Legislative Decree 30/2005, Articles 98-99
perturbation.law_explanation: Articles 98 and 99 of the Italian Industrial Property Code provide legal protection for trade secrets and confidential business information even after the termination of a relationship. The altered text, by stating that all obligations cease upon termination and all rights are relinquished, directly contradicts the enduring protection afforded to trade secrets under Italian law, making the receiving party free to use or disclose the confidential information after the agreement ends, which is illegal under Italian law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: osto pelo agrupamento. 6 — Com a entrada em vigor do presente despacho e até à decisão comunitária sobre o pedido de registo, a indicação referida no n.º 1 goza, a nível nacional, da protecção prevista no n.º 1 do artigo 13.º do Regulamento (CE) n.º 510/2006, do Conselho, de 20 de Março, desig- nadamente contra a sua utilização comercial abusiva ou qualquer outra prática susceptível de induzir o público em erro quanto à verdadeira proveniência, origem, natureza ou qualidade dos produtos. 7 — O agrupamento deve apresentar, junto do GPP, e até 31 de Março de cada ano, um relatório de actividades relativo à gestão da denomina- ção em causa, discriminando, em particular, os produtores que utilizam a denominação, as quantidades beneficiadas e as sanções aplicadas e seus motivos. 8 — O presente despacho produz efeitos desde 8 de Outubro de 2009, data da recepção do pedido formal de protecção junto da Comissão Europeia. 25 de Novembro de 2009. — O Ministro da Agricultura, do Desen- volvimento Rural e das Pescas, António Manuel Soares Serrano. ANEXO I «Carne de Bravo do Ribatejo — DO» I — Descrição do produto Entende-se por «carne de bravo do Ribatejo» a carne proveniente da desmancha de carcaças de bovinos da raça brava de lide. A carne pode apresentar-se como hemi-carcaça ou quartos de carcaça (dianteiros e traseiros), refrigerados; peças de carne de bravo do Ribatejo, acondicio- nadas em saco próprio ou noutros materiais adequados, refrigeradas ou congeladas pelo processo de congelação rápida; preparados de carne de bravo do Ribatejo, produtos picados, moldados, enrolados, em cubos, em tiras ou outras formas, acondicionados em couvettes ou outro material apropriado, em atmosfera controlada, vácuo ou congelados pelo processo de congelação rápida, em que a carne de bravo do Ribatejo representa, no mínimo, e por norma, 95%, em peso, do produto final. a ) Idade dos animais ao abate e peso das carcaças: Idade ao abate Peso da carcaça Carne de novilha brava de lide . . . 18 a 30 meses 100-200 kg Carne de novilho bravo de lide . . . 18 a 30 meses 150-250 kg Carne de vaca brava de lide . . . . . 31 a 60 meses 100-250 kg Carne de toiro bravo de lide . . . . . 31 a 60 meses Inferior a 200 kg b ) Conformação da carcaça: E, U, R. c ) Características da gordura: firme e não exsudativa, coloração branca a amarela, brilhante, de grão fino, suculenta e aromática de sabor agradá- vel, consistente e de elevada qualidade. A gordura intramuscular é muito característica nestas carcaças, apresentando um aspecto geral designado por marmorização (fibras muito finas distribuídas no tecido muscular), contribuindo para a suculência, firmeza e sabor da carne. d ) Características organolépticas: genericamente tenra, com ligeira infiltração de gordura a nível intramuscular, grande suculência, textura suave, detentora de um flaveur característico, não muito intenso. Particu- larmente as características organolépticas distinguidas nesta carne são a cor, textura, sabor e aroma. Cor: vermelho cereja escuro e brilhante, pH: inferior a 6. Textura: a textura é percebida como um misto de maciez, consistência, suculência e mastigabilidade. Sabor e aroma: muito agradável, com muito paladar e um gosto acentuado e persistente. Diário da República, 2.ª série — N.º 234 — 3 de Dezembro de 2009 49053 II — Alimentos para animais A alimentação é feita à base do aproveitamento dos recursos forragei- ros, pasto e palhas e, ainda, da tradicional moinha (glumas e glumelas dos cereais com algum grão à mistura), sempre no interior da área geográ- fica. No Inverno e no Verão, se a quantidade de pastagem é insuficiente para as necessidades dos animais, recorre-se às forragens conservadas (fenos, e ou silagens) obtidas na área geográfica. Pode ainda recorrer-se aos suplementos alimentares (concentrados de sementes naturais, sob a forma de tacos ou granulado), formulados para o efeito, exógenos ou não à região. perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that termination of the agreement affects all obligations, including those related to confidential information, and that all rights are relinquished after the term ends. This directly contradicts the Law Explanation, which states that Italian law provides enduring protection for trade secrets even after termination. Although a relevant Italian law snippet is absent, the combination of the provided Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the clear contradiction between the original and changed text establishes a significant legal contradiction. The provided scraped snippet regarding agricultural regulations in Portugal (Snippet 1) does not relate to trade secrets or intellectual property, and therefore does not directly inform the contradiction analysis.
